rationale: Translated single-line tic-tac-toe-vs-computer prompt into 12 assumptions, 2 inflection points (AI strength, visual style), and an out-of-scope list bounding Builder scope.

Both options well-known in training data; default aligns with casual single-player framing. Unbeatable minimax produces only draws/losses for the human, punishing for a demo. Heuristic (win, block, center, corner, edge) is module-isolated and trivially swappable.

Cascade depth is low and the choice is easily reversible. Default (clean grid, system fonts, hover, win-line highlight, turn indicator, outcome message, restart button) hits the floor of 'looks like a game' without consuming effort better spent on logic. Polish items (animations, custom typography) are explicitly out-of-scope per ledger.

Decomposed into 5 sections: rules-engine (pure logic), ai-opponent (heuristic move selector), ui-render (DOM/CSS presentation), controller-and-shell (index.html + game loop integration), edge-case-testing (final verification). Page-shell folded into controller because it was too thin to own independently and the controller is the natural integration point. Aimed for clean parallelism: rules-engine and ui-render are independent; ai-opponent depends only on rules-engine; controller composes all three.

Four pairwise contracts cover every dependency edge. Contracts pass data shapes (GameState, Outcome, ViewModel) and method signatures, not internal logic. edge-case-testing reuses the four existing contracts as its public surface — no new contract needed.

All six builder outputs were copied to output/integration/ at their Coordinator-specified destinations. index.html already referenced the four JS files and the CSS file by bare filename matching the integration directory layout, so no path rewrites were necessary. Each JS module exposes its expected global (window.RulesEngine, window.AIOpponent, window.UIRender) and the controller consumes those exact names; symbols aligned. node --check confirmed all four JS files parse cleanly. A node smoke test loaded rules-engine + ai-opponent, applied moves, and confirmed AI.chooseMove returned a legal blocking move on a contrived near-win state. Final integration tree byte-matches the sandbox copies that edge-case-testing ran 46/46 passing tests against, so the integrated artifact is the same code that passed verification.

Verified the integrated artifact at output/integration/ against ledger v1 (no amendments). All 12 assumptions confirmed via direct file inspection: single-page index.html (A1), 3x3 grid with 8 canonical winning lines and draw detection (A2), one-human-vs-computer fixed wiring (A3), human=X moves first via createBoard currentPlayer='X' (A4), click-driven cell buttons (A5), in-page Restart button + _resetGame (A6), visible outcome messages 'You win!'/'Computer wins!'/'Draw' in aria-live status (A7), zero matches for fetch/XHR/WebSocket/localStorage/sessionStorage/indexedDB/cookies (A8), no scoreboard variables or DOM (A9), fixed-pixel desktop layout with no @media queries (A10), 'Your turn'/'Computer’s turn'/'Computer thinking...' indicator (A11), CELL_OCCUPIED enforcement plus controller guards block overwrites and undo (A12). All 15 out-of-scope items confirmed absent via targeted greps and structural inspection (no networking, no auth, no storage, no difficulty selector, no symbol/first-move chooser, no undo, no audio, no @keyframes, no variants, no @media, no i18n, no formal a11y audit beyond baseline ARIA, no analytics, no backend). I1 honored: ai-opponent.js implements the exact win/block/center/corner/edge ladder, no minimax. I2 honored: system font stack, clean 3x3 grid, hover state on empty cells, .ttt-cell--win highlight, status text, labelled Restart button, and no elaborate motion design. Edge-case test report shows 46/46 passing with full coverage of winning lines, draw, illegal-input, AI heuristic priorities, renderer behavior, and integration. No spec violations, no concerns flagged.
